# Advanced JavaScript Questions & Answers: 201–300

---

## 201. What is `event.stopPropagation()`?

It stops the event from continuing through the capture or bubble phases.

```js
child.addEventListener("click", event => {
  event.stopPropagation();
  console.log("child clicked");
});

parent.addEventListener("click", () => {
  console.log("parent clicked");
});
```

Clicking `child` logs only:

```txt
child clicked
```

---

## 202. What is `event.stopImmediatePropagation()`?

It stops propagation and prevents other listeners on the same element from running.

```js
button.addEventListener("click", event => {
  event.stopImmediatePropagation();
  console.log("first");
});

button.addEventListener("click", () => {
  console.log("second");
});
```

Output:

```txt
first
```

---

## 203. What is `event.preventDefault()`?

It prevents the browser’s default behavior for an event.

```js
form.addEventListener("submit", event => {
  event.preventDefault();
  console.log("Form handled with JS");
});
```

The form does not submit normally.

---

## 204. What are passive event listeners?

Passive listeners promise not to call `preventDefault`, allowing the browser to optimize scrolling.

```js
window.addEventListener(
  "scroll",
  () => {
    console.log("scrolling");
  },
  { passive: true }
);
```

Useful for `scroll`, `touchstart`, and `touchmove`.

---

## 205. What is the `once` event listener option?

It automatically removes the listener after the first execution.

```js
button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("Runs once");
  },
  { once: true }
);
```

This helps prevent accidental repeated execution and listener leaks.

---

## 206. What is event listener cleanup with `AbortController`?

You can remove event listeners by aborting a signal.

```js
const controller = new AbortController();

button.addEventListener(
  "click",
  () => {
    console.log("clicked");
  },
  { signal: controller.signal }
);

controller.abort();
```

After `abort`, the listener is removed.

---

## 207. What is DOM node memory retention?

A detached DOM node can remain in memory if JavaScript still references it.

```js
let node = document.querySelector("#panel");

node.remove();

// Still retained because `node` references it.
console.log(node.textContent);
```

Set references to `null` when no longer needed.

```js
node = null;
```

---

## 208. How can closures retain DOM nodes?

A closure can keep a DOM node alive even after it is removed.

```js
function setup() {
  const panel = document.querySelector("#panel");

  return function logPanel() {
    console.log(panel.textContent);
  };
}

const logger = setup();

document.querySelector("#panel").remove();
```

The removed DOM node can still be retained through `logger`.

---

## 209. What is `MutationObserver`?

It watches changes to the DOM.

```js
const observer = new MutationObserver(records => {
  console.log(records);
});

observer.observe(document.body, {
  childList: true,
  subtree: true
});

document.body.append("Hello");
```

Remember to disconnect observers when finished.

```js
observer.disconnect();
```

---

## 210. What is `IntersectionObserver`?

It detects when an element enters or leaves the viewport or another root.

```js
const observer = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
  for (const entry of entries) {
    if (entry.isIntersecting) {
      console.log("Visible");
    }
  }
});

observer.observe(document.querySelector("#target"));
```

Useful for lazy loading and infinite scroll.

---

## 211. What is `ResizeObserver`?

It observes size changes of an element.

```js
const observer = new ResizeObserver(entries => {
  for (const entry of entries) {
    console.log(entry.contentRect.width);
  }
});

observer.observe(document.querySelector("#box"));
```

Useful for responsive components.

---

## 212. Why can observers cause memory leaks?

Observers keep references to callbacks and observed targets.

```js
const observer = new ResizeObserver(() => {
  console.log("resize");
});

observer.observe(element);

// If element is removed but observer is not disconnected,
// memory may be retained.
```

Cleanup:

```js
observer.unobserve(element);
observer.disconnect();
```

---

## 213. What is shadow DOM?

Shadow DOM creates an encapsulated DOM tree attached to an element.

```js
const host = document.querySelector("#host");

const shadow = host.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });

shadow.innerHTML = `
  <style>
    p { color: red; }
  </style>
  <p>Hello from shadow DOM</p>
`;
```

Styles and DOM structure are isolated from the main document.

---

## 214. What is the difference between open and closed shadow DOM?

Open shadow DOM is accessible through `element.shadowRoot`. Closed shadow DOM is not.

```js
const openRoot = element.attachShadow({ mode: "open" });

console.log(element.shadowRoot === openRoot); // true
```

Closed:

```js
const closedRoot = element.attachShadow({ mode: "closed" });

console.log(element.shadowRoot); // null
```

---

## 215. What is event retargeting in shadow DOM?

Events crossing a shadow boundary may expose the host as the target instead of internal elements.

```js
host.addEventListener("click", event => {
  console.log(event.target);
});
```

If a button inside shadow DOM is clicked, outside listeners may see the host as the target.

---

## 216. What is `event.composedPath()`?

It returns the full event path, including shadow DOM boundaries when allowed.

```js
host.addEventListener("click", event => {
  console.log(event.composedPath());
});
```

Useful when working with web components.

---

## 217. What is a custom element?

A custom element defines a reusable HTML element with JavaScript.

```js
class UserCard extends HTMLElement {
  connectedCallback() {
    this.innerHTML = `<p>User card</p>`;
  }
}

customElements.define("user-card", UserCard);
```

Usage:

```html
<user-card></user-card>
```

---

## 218. What is `connectedCallback`?

It runs when a custom element is inserted into the DOM.

```js
class AppBox extends HTMLElement {
  connectedCallback() {
    console.log("Added to page");
  }
}

customElements.define("app-box", AppBox);
```

Useful for setup.

---

## 219. What is `disconnectedCallback`?

It runs when a custom element is removed from the DOM.

```js
class TimerBox extends HTMLElement {
  connectedCallback() {
    this.id = setInterval(() => console.log("tick"), 1000);
  }

  disconnectedCallback() {
    clearInterval(this.id);
  }
}

customElements.define("timer-box", TimerBox);
```

Useful for cleanup.

---

## 220. What is `attributeChangedCallback`?

It runs when observed attributes change.

```js
class NameTag extends HTMLElement {
  static observedAttributes = ["name"];

  attributeChangedCallback(name, oldValue, newValue) {
    console.log(`${name}: ${oldValue} -> ${newValue}`);
  }
}

customElements.define("name-tag", NameTag);
```

---

## 221. What is CORS?

CORS controls whether a browser allows JavaScript from one origin to read responses from another origin.

```js
fetch("https://api.example.com/data")
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(console.log);
```

The server must send proper CORS headers, such as:

```txt
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://your-site.com
```

---

## 222. What is the same-origin policy?

It restricts one origin from reading sensitive data from another origin.

```js
fetch("https://another-site.com/private")
  .then(res => res.text())
  .then(console.log);
```

The browser may block access unless CORS allows it.

An origin is:

```txt
scheme + host + port
```

---

## 223. What is a preflight request?

For certain cross-origin requests, the browser sends an `OPTIONS` request first.

```js
fetch("https://api.example.com/users", {
  method: "PUT",
  headers: {
    "Content-Type": "application/json"
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Ava" })
});
```

The browser checks whether the server allows the method and headers.

---

## 224. What are credentials in fetch?

Credentials include cookies, HTTP auth, and TLS client certificates.

```js
fetch("https://api.example.com/profile", {
  credentials: "include"
});
```

For cross-origin credentialed requests, the server must explicitly allow credentials.

---

## 225. What is XSS?

XSS means Cross-Site Scripting. It happens when untrusted data is executed as code.

```js
const userInput = `<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>`;

document.body.innerHTML = userInput; // dangerous
```

Safer:

```js
document.body.textContent = userInput;
```

---

## 226. Why is `innerHTML` dangerous?

It parses strings as HTML, which can execute attacker-controlled content.

```js
function renderComment(comment) {
  comments.innerHTML = comment; // unsafe
}
```

Safer:

```js
function renderComment(comment) {
  comments.textContent = comment;
}
```

---

## 227. What is CSP?

Content Security Policy is a browser security mechanism that restricts which scripts, styles, images, and other resources can load.

```html
<meta
  http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy"
  content="script-src 'self'; object-src 'none'"
>
```

A strong CSP can reduce XSS impact.

---

## 228. What is CSRF?

CSRF tricks a user’s browser into sending an authenticated request to another site.

```html
<form action="https://bank.example/transfer" method="POST">
  <input name="amount" value="1000">
</form>
```

Mitigations include SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, and checking origin headers.

---

## 229. What is `SameSite` cookie behavior?

`SameSite` controls whether cookies are sent with cross-site requests.

```txt
Set-Cookie: session=abc; SameSite=Lax; Secure; HttpOnly
```

Common values:

```txt
Strict
Lax
None
```

`SameSite=None` requires `Secure`.

---

## 230. Why use `HttpOnly` cookies?

`HttpOnly` prevents JavaScript from reading cookies.

```txt
Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
```

This reduces damage from XSS because attacker scripts cannot directly steal the cookie.

---

## 231. What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?

`localStorage` persists across browser sessions. `sessionStorage` lasts for one tab session.

```js
localStorage.setItem("theme", "dark");
sessionStorage.setItem("draft", "hello");
```

Both are synchronous and can block the main thread.

---

## 232. Why can localStorage be risky?

It is readable by JavaScript, so XSS can steal sensitive data.

```js
localStorage.setItem("token", "secret-token");

// Any injected script can read:
console.log(localStorage.getItem("token"));
```

Avoid storing highly sensitive tokens in localStorage.

---

## 233. What is IndexedDB?

IndexedDB is an asynchronous browser database for structured data.

```js
const request = indexedDB.open("app-db", 1);

request.onupgradeneeded = event => {
  const db = event.target.result;
  db.createObjectStore("users", { keyPath: "id" });
};
```

It is better than localStorage for larger client-side data.

---

## 234. What is Cache Storage?

Cache Storage stores request-response pairs, often used by service workers.

```js
const cache = await caches.open("v1");

await cache.add("/app.js");

const response = await cache.match("/app.js");
```

Useful for offline apps and performance.

---

## 235. What is a service worker?

A service worker is a background script that can intercept network requests, cache assets, and enable offline behavior.

```js
navigator.serviceWorker.register("/sw.js");
```

Inside `sw.js`:

```js
self.addEventListener("fetch", event => {
  event.respondWith(fetch(event.request));
});
```

---

## 236. What is the service worker lifecycle?

A service worker goes through install, activate, and fetch phases.

```js
self.addEventListener("install", event => {
  console.log("install");
});

self.addEventListener("activate", event => {
  console.log("activate");
});

self.addEventListener("fetch", event => {
  console.log("fetch", event.request.url);
});
```

---

## 237. Why can service worker updates be confusing?

A new service worker may install but wait until old pages close.

```js
self.addEventListener("install", event => {
  self.skipWaiting();
});

self.addEventListener("activate", event => {
  clients.claim();
});
```

`skipWaiting` and `clients.claim` can force quicker activation, but must be used carefully.

---

## 238. What is a stale-while-revalidate strategy?

Serve cached data immediately, then update the cache in the background.

```js
self.addEventListener("fetch", event => {
  event.respondWith(
    caches.open("v1").then(async cache => {
      const cached = await cache.match(event.request);

      const network = fetch(event.request).then(response => {
        cache.put(event.request, response.clone());
        return response;
      });

      return cached || network;
    })
  );
});
```

This improves perceived speed.

---

## 239. Why must you clone a Response before caching it?

A response body stream can be consumed only once.

```js
const response = await fetch("/data");

cache.put("/data", response.clone());

const data = await response.json();
```

Without `clone`, one consumer may consume the body and leave nothing for the other.

---

## 240. What is a stream body lock?

When you call `getReader`, the stream becomes locked to that reader.

```js
const response = await fetch("/large-file");

const reader = response.body.getReader();

console.log(response.body.locked); // true
```

You cannot attach another reader until the lock is released.

---

## 241. What is Node.js event loop?

Node.js uses an event loop provided by libuv to handle timers, I/O callbacks, check callbacks, close callbacks, and more.

```js
const fs = require("fs");

setTimeout(() => console.log("timer"), 0);

fs.readFile(__filename, () => {
  console.log("file read");
});
```

Node’s event loop differs from the browser event loop.

---

## 242. What are Node.js event loop phases?

Common phases include timers, pending callbacks, poll, check, and close callbacks.

```js
setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout"), 0);

setImmediate(() => console.log("immediate"));
```

Ordering can depend on context, especially whether code runs inside an I/O callback.

---

## 243. What is `process.nextTick`?

`process.nextTick` schedules a callback before promise microtasks in Node.js.

```js
process.nextTick(() => console.log("nextTick"));

Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log("promise"));

console.log("sync");
```

Typical output:

```txt
sync
nextTick
promise
```

Overusing it can starve the event loop.

---

## 244. How can `process.nextTick` starve Node.js?

Recursive `nextTick` callbacks can prevent I/O from running.

```js
function loop() {
  process.nextTick(loop);
}

loop();

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log("timer");
}, 0);
```

The timer may never execute.

---

## 245. What is `setImmediate`?

`setImmediate` schedules a callback in the check phase of Node’s event loop.

```js
setImmediate(() => {
  console.log("immediate");
});

setTimeout(() => {
  console.log("timeout");
}, 0);
```

Ordering outside I/O can vary.

---

## 246. What is the difference between `setImmediate` and `setTimeout(0)`?

`setTimeout(0)` runs in the timers phase. `setImmediate` runs in the check phase.

```js
const fs = require("fs");

fs.readFile(__filename, () => {
  setTimeout(() => console.log("timeout"), 0);
  setImmediate(() => console.log("immediate"));
});
```

Inside an I/O callback, `setImmediate` usually runs before `setTimeout(0)`.

---

## 247. What is the Node.js thread pool?

Node uses a thread pool for some expensive or blocking operations, such as file system work, DNS, compression, and crypto.

```js
const crypto = require("crypto");

crypto.pbkdf2("password", "salt", 100000, 64, "sha512", () => {
  console.log("done");
});
```

This work runs outside the main JavaScript thread.

---

## 248. Is Node.js truly single-threaded?

JavaScript execution mostly runs on one main thread, but Node uses worker threads, libuv thread pool, and OS-level async I/O.

```js
const { Worker } = require("worker_threads");

new Worker(`
  const { parentPort } = require("worker_threads");
  parentPort.postMessage("hello");
`, { eval: true });
```

So Node.js is not purely single-threaded internally.

---

## 249. What are Node.js worker threads?

Worker threads allow CPU-heavy JavaScript to run in parallel.

```js
const { Worker } = require("worker_threads");

const worker = new Worker("./worker.js");

worker.on("message", message => {
  console.log(message);
});
```

They are useful for CPU-bound workloads.

---

## 250. What is the difference between child process and worker thread?

A child process has separate memory and its own process. A worker thread shares the same process but has separate JS execution.

```js
const { fork } = require("child_process");

const child = fork("./child.js");

child.send({ hello: "world" });
```

Worker threads are lighter than processes but require careful handling with shared memory.

---

## 251. What is a Node.js stream?

A stream processes data piece by piece instead of loading it all into memory.

```js
const fs = require("fs");

fs.createReadStream("large.txt")
  .on("data", chunk => {
    console.log(chunk.length);
  });
```

Useful for large files, network data, and pipelines.

---

## 252. What is stream backpressure in Node.js?

Backpressure occurs when the writable side cannot keep up with the readable side.

```js
const readable = fs.createReadStream("large.txt");
const writable = fs.createWriteStream("copy.txt");

readable.pipe(writable);
```

`pipe` handles backpressure automatically.

---

## 253. What is `pipeline` in Node.js?

`pipeline` safely connects streams and handles errors.

```js
const { pipeline } = require("stream/promises");
const fs = require("fs");

await pipeline(
  fs.createReadStream("input.txt"),
  fs.createWriteStream("output.txt")
);
```

Prefer it over manual `.pipe()` when error handling matters.

---

## 254. What is Buffer in Node.js?

`Buffer` represents raw binary data.

```js
const buf = Buffer.from("hello");

console.log(buf);
console.log(buf.toString()); // "hello"
```

Buffers are common in file, network, and crypto operations.

---

## 255. What is the difference between Buffer and ArrayBuffer?

`ArrayBuffer` is standard JavaScript binary memory. `Buffer` is Node.js-specific and extends `Uint8Array`.

```js
const buffer = Buffer.from("hello");

console.log(buffer instanceof Uint8Array); // true
```

Modern Node APIs often interoperate with both.

---

## 256. What is CommonJS module caching?

CommonJS modules are executed once and then cached.

```js
// counter.js
let count = 0;
count++;

module.exports = count;
```

```js
const a = require("./counter");
const b = require("./counter");

console.log(a, b); // 1 1
```

The module body does not rerun on the second `require`.

---

## 257. How do CommonJS circular dependencies behave?

CommonJS returns partially initialized exports during cycles.

```js
// a.js
exports.name = "A";
const b = require("./b");
console.log(b.name);

// b.js
exports.name = "B";
const a = require("./a");
console.log(a.name);
```

A module may observe another module before it has finished executing.

---

## 258. What is dynamic import in Node.js?

Dynamic `import()` loads ESM asynchronously.

```js
async function load() {
  const fs = await import("node:fs/promises");

  const text = await fs.readFile("file.txt", "utf8");
  console.log(text);
}

load();
```

It works in both ESM and CommonJS contexts.

---

## 259. What is module resolution?

Module resolution determines what file or package an import refers to.

```js
import { helper } from "./utils/helper.js";
import express from "express";
```

Relative imports resolve from the current file. Bare specifiers resolve through package rules.

---

## 260. Why do ESM imports often need file extensions?

In many ESM environments, relative imports must include the exact file extension.

```js
import { add } from "./math.js";
```

This avoids ambiguous resolution and aligns better with browser module loading.

---

## 261. What is the `exports` field in `package.json`?

It controls which files a package exposes.

```json
{
  "name": "my-lib",
  "exports": {
    ".": "./dist/index.js",
    "./utils": "./dist/utils.js"
  }
}
```

Consumers can import only exported paths.

---

## 262. What is conditional exports?

Conditional exports provide different files for different environments.

```json
{
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "import": "./esm/index.js",
      "require": "./cjs/index.cjs",
      "browser": "./browser/index.js"
    }
  }
}
```

This allows packages to support ESM, CommonJS, Node, and browser builds.

---

## 263. What is tree shaking?

Tree shaking removes unused exports from bundled code.

```js
// utils.js
export function used() {}
export function unused() {}

// app.js
import { used } from "./utils.js";

used();
```

A bundler may remove `unused` if it can prove it has no side effects.

---

## 264. Why can side effects break tree shaking?

If importing a module runs code, the bundler may need to keep it.

```js
// setup.js
console.log("side effect");

export function helper() {}
```

Even if `helper` is unused, the module’s top-level code may matter.

---

## 265. What is the `sideEffects` package.json field?

It tells bundlers whether files have top-level side effects.

```json
{
  "sideEffects": false
}
```

Or selectively:

```json
{
  "sideEffects": [
    "*.css",
    "./src/polyfills.js"
  ]
}
```

Incorrectly setting this can break applications.

---

## 266. What is code splitting?

Code splitting breaks an app into smaller chunks loaded on demand.

```js
button.addEventListener("click", async () => {
  const module = await import("./heavy-feature.js");

  module.run();
});
```

This reduces initial load cost.

---

## 267. What is lazy loading?

Lazy loading delays loading a resource until needed.

```js
const image = document.querySelector("img");

image.loading = "lazy";
```

For JavaScript:

```js
const chart = await import("./chart.js");
```

---

## 268. What is hydration?

Hydration attaches JavaScript behavior to server-rendered HTML.

```js
// Server sends HTML:
<div id="app"><button>Click</button></div>
```

Client JS attaches event handlers:

```js
document.querySelector("button").addEventListener("click", () => {
  console.log("clicked");
});
```

Frameworks automate this process.

---

## 269. What is hydration mismatch?

A hydration mismatch happens when server-rendered HTML differs from the client’s expected output.

```js
function App() {
  return `<p>${Date.now()}</p>`;
}
```

The server and client produce different timestamps, causing mismatch.

Better:

```js
function App() {
  return `<p data-client-time></p>`;
}
```

Then fill it after client load.

---

## 270. What is progressive enhancement?

Progressive enhancement means the app works basically without JavaScript, then improves with JavaScript.

```html
<form action="/search" method="GET">
  <input name="q">
  <button>Search</button>
</form>
```

JavaScript can enhance it:

```js
form.addEventListener("submit", event => {
  event.preventDefault();
  // AJAX search
});
```

---

## 271. What is graceful degradation?

Graceful degradation starts with a full-featured experience and ensures acceptable fallback if features fail.

```js
if ("serviceWorker" in navigator) {
  navigator.serviceWorker.register("/sw.js");
} else {
  console.log("Offline support unavailable");
}
```

The app still works without service workers.

---

## 272. What is feature detection?

Feature detection checks whether an API exists before using it.

```js
if ("IntersectionObserver" in window) {
  // use IntersectionObserver
} else {
  // fallback
}
```

This is better than relying only on browser detection.

---

## 273. What is polyfill?

A polyfill implements a missing API in older environments.

```js
if (!Array.prototype.includes) {
  Array.prototype.includes = function (value) {
    return this.indexOf(value) !== -1;
  };
}
```

Polyfills modify or add APIs to match newer behavior.

---

## 274. What is transpilation?

Transpilation converts newer JavaScript syntax into older JavaScript syntax.

```js
const add = (a, b) => a + b;
```

Can become:

```js
var add = function (a, b) {
  return a + b;
};
```

Tools like Babel perform transpilation.

---

## 275. What is the difference between polyfill and transpilation?

Transpilation changes syntax. Polyfills add runtime APIs.

```js
// Syntax needing transpilation:
const fn = () => {};
```

```js
// API needing polyfill:
Promise.any([p1, p2]);
```

Old environments may need both.

---

## 276. What is source map?

A source map maps bundled/transpiled code back to original source code.

```js
//# sourceMappingURL=app.js.map
```

It helps debugging production errors.

---

## 277. What is minification?

Minification reduces code size by removing whitespace and shortening names.

```js
function add(firstNumber, secondNumber) {
  return firstNumber + secondNumber;
}
```

May become:

```js
function add(n,d){return n+d}
```

This improves network performance.

---

## 278. What is dead code elimination?

It removes code that cannot affect program output.

```js
if (false) {
  console.log("never runs");
}
```

A bundler or minifier can remove that block.

---

## 279. What is constant folding?

Constant folding evaluates constant expressions during build optimization.

```js
const seconds = 60 * 60;
```

Can become:

```js
const seconds = 3600;
```

This reduces runtime work.

---

## 280. What is inlining?

Inlining replaces a function call with the function body.

```js
function square(x) {
  return x * x;
}

const y = square(4);
```

An optimizer may treat it like:

```js
const y = 4 * 4;
```

Inlining can improve speed but may increase code size.

---

## 281. What is escape analysis?

Escape analysis determines whether an object can be kept on the stack or optimized away.

```js
function pointSum() {
  const point = { x: 1, y: 2 };

  return point.x + point.y;
}
```

If `point` does not escape the function, an engine may avoid allocating it as a normal heap object.

---

## 282. What does it mean for an object to escape?

An object escapes when it can be accessed outside its local scope.

```js
function createPoint() {
  const point = { x: 1, y: 2 };

  return point;
}

const p = createPoint();
```

Because `point` is returned, it must survive after the function returns.

---

## 283. What is scalar replacement?

Scalar replacement breaks an object into separate primitive values during optimization.

```js
function sumPoint() {
  const point = { x: 10, y: 20 };

  return point.x + point.y;
}
```

The engine may optimize this as if it were:

```js
const x = 10;
const y = 20;
return x + y;
```

---

## 284. What is dead store elimination?

It removes assignments whose values are never read.

```js
let x = 1;
x = 2;

console.log(x);
```

The assignment `x = 1` may be optimized away.

---

## 285. What is bounds check elimination?

Engines can remove repeated array bounds checks when they can prove indexes are safe.

```js
function sum(arr) {
  let total = 0;

  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    total += arr[i];
  }

  return total;
}
```

The loop condition helps the optimizer reason about valid indexes.

---

## 286. Why can changing array length hurt optimization?

Changing array length inside a loop invalidates assumptions.

```js
function process(arr) {
  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    arr.push(i);
  }
}
```

The loop may never finish or may prevent useful optimizations.

---

## 287. Why can mixed arrays be slower?

Arrays with consistent element types are easier to optimize.

```js
const numbers = [1, 2, 3];

numbers.push("four");
```

The engine may need to transition the array representation to handle mixed values.

---

## 288. What are SMI integers?

SMI means small integer. Engines can represent small integers more efficiently than general numbers.

```js
const x = 42;
const y = 100;
```

If values remain small integers, arithmetic may be optimized.

But:

```js
const z = 1.5;
```

Floating-point values may require a different representation.

---

## 289. What is boxing in engine optimization?

Boxing wraps a primitive in an object-like representation when needed.

```js
const n = 42;

console.log(n.toString());
```

The engine may need object-like behavior for method access, though it can often optimize this away.

---

## 290. Why can `try...catch` affect optimization?

Historically, `try...catch` made optimization harder because control flow and error handling were complex. Modern engines handle it better, but hot code should still avoid unnecessary exception-based control flow.

```js
function parse(value) {
  try {
    return JSON.parse(value);
  } catch {
    return null;
  }
}
```

This is fine for actual exceptional cases, but not ideal for normal branching.

---

## 291. Why are exceptions expensive?

Throwing captures stack information and unwinds control flow.

```js
function findUser(users, id) {
  for (const user of users) {
    if (user.id === id) return user;
  }

  throw new Error("User not found");
}
```

For expected misses, returning `null` may be better:

```js
function findUser(users, id) {
  return users.find(user => user.id === id) ?? null;
}
```

---

## 292. What is a hot path?

A hot path is code executed frequently enough that performance matters.

```js
function renderItem(item) {
  return item.price * item.quantity;
}

for (const item of items) {
  renderItem(item);
}
```

Small inefficiencies in hot paths can become significant.

---

## 293. What is a cold path?

A cold path is code that runs rarely.

```js
function handleRequest(req) {
  if (!req.user) {
    return showLoginError(); // cold path
  }

  return processRequest(req); // hot path
}
```

Optimize hot paths first. Keep cold paths readable.

---

## 294. What is premature optimization?

Premature optimization means making code more complex for performance without evidence.

```js
// Complex custom cache before measuring actual need
const cache = new Map();
```

Better approach:

```js
console.time("operation");
// run operation
console.timeEnd("operation");
```

Measure first, then optimize.

---

## 295. What is benchmarking bias?

Benchmarking bias happens when a test does not represent real-world behavior.

```js
console.time("loop");

for (let i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) {
  Math.sqrt(i);
}

console.timeEnd("loop");
```

Microbenchmarks can be distorted by JIT warmup, dead code elimination, and unrealistic inputs.

---

## 296. Why should benchmarks warm up code?

Engines optimize after observing repeated execution.

```js
function add(a, b) {
  return a + b;
}

// Warmup
for (let i = 0; i < 10000; i++) {
  add(i, i);
}

// Measure after warmup
console.time("add");
for (let i = 0; i < 100000; i++) {
  add(i, i);
}
console.timeEnd("add");
```

Without warmup, you may measure interpreter speed rather than optimized speed.

---

## 297. Why can benchmark results change between browsers?

Different engines use different parsers, interpreters, JIT compilers, garbage collectors, and optimization heuristics.

```js
function test(obj) {
  return obj.x + obj.y;
}
```

One engine may optimize this better than another depending on object shapes and runtime feedback.

---

## 298. What is garbage collection pause?

A GC pause is time spent tracing, marking, sweeping, compacting, or moving memory.

```js
const items = [];

setInterval(() => {
  items.push(new Array(10000).fill("x"));
}, 10);
```

Heavy allocation can increase GC frequency and cause pauses.

---

## 299. How can you reduce GC pressure?

Avoid unnecessary allocations in hot paths.

```js
// More allocations
function update(items) {
  return items.map(item => ({
    ...item,
    active: true
  }));
}
```

In performance-critical code, reuse objects carefully:

```js
function updateInto(target, source) {
  target.id = source.id;
  target.active = true;
  return target;
}
```

Do this only when mutation is safe and measured.

---

## 300. What is engine-level JavaScript performance really about?

It is about helping the engine keep stable assumptions: stable object shapes, predictable types, packed arrays, limited allocations, clean async scheduling, and avoiding accidental retention.

```js
function createUser(name, age) {
  return {
    name,
    age,
    active: true
  };
}

const users = [
  createUser("Ava", 30),
  createUser("Mia", 25),
  createUser("Noah", 28)
];

function getNames(users) {
  const names = [];

  for (const user of users) {
    names.push(user.name);
  }

  return names;
}

console.log(getNames(users));
```

This code is friendly to engines because objects are created with the same shape, properties are accessed predictably, arrays are dense, and there is no unnecessary type mixing.
